content: The addition of arginine at a concentration of 0.5 M will reduce the viscosity of a bispecific antibody solution at 180 mg/ml by at least 20%.
explanation: Arginine is known to interact with proteins in a way that can alter their surface properties and reduce intermolecular interactions, thus potentially decreasing viscosity. This hypothesis is grounded in the understanding of protein chemistry and solvent interactions.
provenance: Studies have shown that arginine can reduce the viscosity of highly concentrated protein solutions through its chaotropic effects (Shire et al., 2004).
Arginine is commonly used as a stabilizing agent in protein formulation (Yamaguchi et al., 2010).

content: The addition of arginine to a bispecific antibody solution at a concentration of 180 mg/ml will reduce its viscosity by at least 20% compared to a control solution without arginine.
explanation: Arginine is known to interact with protein molecules, potentially altering their interaction and aggregation behavior, which can lead to reduced viscosity in high-concentration protein solutions.
provenance: Studies have shown that arginine can reduce protein-protein interactions and aggregation, which are key factors contributing to high viscosity in antibody solutions (Shire et al., 2004, Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences).

content: The addition of arginine hydrochloride at a concentration of 0.5 M will reduce the viscosity of a bispecific antibody solution at 180 mg/ml by at least 20%.
explanation: Arginine hydrochloride is known to reduce protein-protein interactions which are a major contributor to increased viscosity in protein solutions. This hypothesis is based on previous studies showing that arginine can effectively lower the viscosity of therapeutic proteins by disrupting intermolecular interactions.
provenance: Recent studies on monoclonal antibodies have demonstrated the effectiveness of arginine in lowering solution viscosity. (Yadav et al., Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 2012)
Arginine hydrochloride has been documented as a stabilizing agent for high-concentration protein formulations. (Shire et al., Biotechnology and Bioengineering, 2004)

Past studies have shown that excipients like arginine can effectively reduce the viscosity of high concentration protein solutions.

content: The addition of arginine hydrochloride at a concentration of 50 mM will reduce the viscosity of a bispecific antibody solution at 180 mg/ml by at least 20% compared to a control solution without arginine hydrochloride.
explanation: Arginine hydrochloride is known to reduce protein-protein interactions that contribute to high viscosity in protein solutions. By disrupting these interactions, it is likely to lower the viscosity of the antibody solution.

content: The addition of a specific excipient, such as arginine, will decrease the viscosity of a bispecific antibody solution at a concentration of 180 mg/ml.
explanation: Arginine is known to reduce protein-protein interactions and improve solubility, which can lower the viscosity of protein solutions.

content: Adding low concentrations of polysorbate 80 to a bispecific antibody solution at 180 mg/ml will reduce the solution's viscosity.

content: Adding arginine to a bispecific antibody solution at a concentration of 180 mg/ml will reduce its viscosity by at least 10%.

content: Adding arginine at a concentration of 100 mM to the formulation will reduce the viscosity of a bispecific antibody solution at 180 mg/ml.
